name: Platform-neutral trust anchor governance
consequences: Adopting a platform-neutral trust governance reduces single-vendor dependence by enabling fallback authentication paths independent of Apple/Google signals. Downstream, it compels cross-agency coordination and new certification regimes, and it may slow procurement cycles as standards mature and interoperability tests are conducted.
options: Establish a platform-agnostic trust framework anchored in open signals and standard interfaces to support fallback authentication.
Incorporate platform-neutral trust criteria into MitID-related procurement and certification processes to diversify trust anchors.
Institute a sunset clause on exclusive platform integrity signals with a defined migration path to alternatives.
review: Platform sovereignty vs. procurement agility. Weakness: The options may slow procurement and coordination across agencies.

name: Open-standards demonstrator program for fallback authentication
consequences: Developing non-production demonstrators will yield concrete proof that standards-based fallbacks are technically feasible without OS-level trust anchors. Downstream, these efforts may not translate into production deployments and could divert technical resources away from near-term implementation.
options: Develop a non-production demonstrator authenticating against a simulated identity environment using FIDO2/WebAuthn and OpenID Connect.
Prototype a browser-based or hardware-token fallback path to reduce dependence on mobile OS trust anchors.
Implement a cross-platform credential wallet pilot that distributes trust across vendors and reduces single-source risk.
review: Demonstration-focused innovation vs. production-readiness. Weakness: Demonstrations may not translate into implementable procurement-ready solutions.

name: Procurement-driven platform-neutrality and AltID alignment
consequences: Embedding platform-neutrality in AltID procurement will pressure operators to diversify trust anchors and reduce lock-in risks. Downstream, incumbents may resist and procurement cycles may lengthen as evaluations adjust to new criteria.
options: Embed platform-neutrality requirements into AltID procurement language and evaluation criteria.
Create a cross-agency standard for vendor diversification and assurance of fallback access in procurements.
Institute milestone-based procurement gating that ties budget releases to platform-neutral milestones.
review: Platform-neutral goals vs. incumbent advantages. Weakness: Procurement changes may face resistance from legacy operators.

name: Coalition-building and regulatory engagement
consequences: Formalizing a broad coalition builds political legitimacy for resilience investments and procures space for longer-term reform. Downstream, the process can be slow due to differing priorities and power dynamics, potentially delaying concrete policy outcomes.
options: Formalize a multi-stakeholder coalition spanning civil society, privacy advocates, and academic researchers to advocate resilience.
Coordinate targeted engagement with Folketinget and EU policymakers to secure supportive governance.
Publish a transparent impact assessment and risk narrative to build legitimacy.
review: Coalition-building vs. political fragmentation. Weakness: Aligning diverse stakeholders may delay decision gates.

name: EU and standards alignment for interoperability
consequences: Engaging with EU workstreams helps harmonize Danish fallback requirements with European interoperability standards. Downstream, national constraints may need adaptation, and there may be friction with domestic procurement cycles.
options: Engage EU working groups to influence European Digital Identity Wallet requirements for platform-neutral access.
Contribute Danish case studies to EU guidelines demonstrating fallback authentication feasibility.
Align national procurement language with EU interoperability standards to ease cross-border adoption.
review: EU alignment vs. national sovereignty. Weakness: EU standards may not perfectly map to Danish procurement realities.

name: Continuity and resilience planning integration with procurement and regulatory oversight
consequences: Integrating continuity planning into governance makes resilience a formal recurring activity rather than an ad hoc exercise. Downstream, this may increase ongoing costs and require sustained cross-agency engagement to maintain the framework.
options: Integrate continuity-of-service requirements into risk governance and procurement oversight.
Develop a formal risk register with explicit transition plans and blockers for platform-neutral strategies.
Pilot a shared resilience testing framework across agencies to validate fallback paths.
review: Continuity planning vs. resource constraints. Weakness: Ongoing resilience efforts may strain budgets and staffing.

name: Procurement-language requirements for platform-neutral fallback paths
consequences: Explicit procurement criteria incentivize suppliers to develop and certify platform-neutral fallback authentication paths. This reduces single-vendor risk and creates a long-term resilience baseline for Danish digital identity. Trade-offs include longer procurement cycles, potential delays to policy milestones, and the need for new certification processes to verify interoperability.
options: Embed platform-neutral fallback requirements into AltID procurement language to ensure future vendors compete on interoperability, security, and resilience.
Mandate independent certification for any fallback-identity provider demonstrating conformity with open standards and verifiable security claims.
Incorporate phased rollout criteria that reward early adoption of safe fallback paths while maintaining rigorous privacy safeguards.
review: Platform-neutral fallback in procurement vs. speed of contracting. Weakness: The options may underestimate the operational costs of changing procurement templates and certification regimes.

name: Independent trust anchor demonstrations across privacy-respecting platforms
consequences: Demonstrations across multiple platforms will surface practical interoperability gaps and verify the viability of platform-neutral trust anchors. Early findings could attract cross-sector support and funding for broader demonstrations. Risks include coordination complexity, schedule slippage, and incremental costs.
options: Commission a multi-platform demonstration of a platform-neutral trust anchor using GrapheneOS and hardware tokens to validate interoperability.
Partner with academic labs to assess device telemetry and integrity signals independent of Apple/Google ecosystems.
Publish a technical white paper detailing open, auditable trust-chains that do not rely on vendor-controlled signals.
review: Independent trust anchor demonstrations vs. reliance on platform signals. Weakness: The options may overlook governance overhead and the risk of vendor coordination delays.

name: EU-aligned interoperability and NSIS resilience clauses in AltID procurement
consequences: EU-aligned standards will push cross-border interoperability and resilience, reducing domestic lock-in and enabling broader vendor ecosystems. This can accelerate adoption of fallback paths but may constrain national-specific procurement flexibilities. Potential mismatch between EU timelines and Danish policy cadence could create friction.
options: Integrate NSIS-compliant resilience clauses into AltID-related standards to ensure cross-border interoperability and fallback capability.
Coordinate with EU bodies to reflect platform-neutral access requirements in European Digital Identity Wallet guidelines.
Advocate for common certification criteria across member states to accelerate procurement adoption of fallback authentication.
review: EU-aligned standards vs. national procurement flexibility. Weakness: The options may overstate EU alignment while underestimating national regulatory friction and sovereignty concerns.

name: Coalition-led risk communication framing around supplier concentration and continuity
consequences: Risk framing will reposition identity continuity as essential national infrastructure, increasing public and political support for platform-neutral approaches. It can influence procurement incentives toward resilience, but may provoke pushback from incumbents who benefit from the status quo. Messaging risk includes potential oversimplification of technical challenges.
options: Launch a public-trust campaign that frames identity continuity as essential national infrastructure and strategic autonomy.
Publish scenario analyses showing outage risks and recovery timelines to inform policy debate and procurement criteria.
Organize stakeholder roundtables with civil society, SMEs, and local government to surface procurement-friction pain points.
review: Platform-centric messaging vs. political inertia. Weakness: The options may not directly shift procurement incentives without credible data.

name: Open-source interoperability baseline for AltID and fallback pathways
consequences: Open-source baseline promotes transparency and vendor competition, enabling independent audits. It helps secure interoperability across devices and browsers, building confidence among stakeholders. However, sustaining open-source maintenance and governance requires ongoing funding and community stewardship.
options: Establish an open-source compliance baseline for wallet interoperability to encourage vendor competition and auditability.
Create a public reference implementation of a fallback-authentication flow that can be audited by independent researchers.
Sponsor community-driven testing events that validate interoperability across devices, browsers, and operating systems.
review: Open-source baseline vs proprietary lock-in. Weakness: The options may not guarantee purchasing incentives for open-source ecosystems or ensure sustained maintenance.

name: National governance framework for platform-agnostic identity trust chains
consequences: National governance frameworks clarify roles and accountability, reducing ambiguity in crisis scenarios and ensuring ongoing oversight of platform-agnostic trust chains. They facilitate consistent procurement and risk management, but add administrative overhead and potential delays in decision-making.
options: Define clear accountability lines between Digitaliseringsstyrelsen, operators, and external auditors for platform-agnostic trust chains.
Institute quarterly risk reviews and red-team exercises to test fallback authentication under simulated disruption scenarios.
Publish public governance norms requiring ongoing certification updates aligned with EU identity-wallet evolutions.
review: Platform-agnostic governance vs operational agility. Weakness: The options may slow decision-making due to governance overhead and policy alignment requirements.

name: Procurement-driven platform-neutral fallback integration
consequences: Pulling this lever enforces a platform-neutral fallback as a contractual requirement, creating a clear path toward supplier diversification. Downstream, it broadens the potential vendor pool and increases resilience to platform outages, but it can extend procurement cycles and heighten negotiation complexity with incumbents.
options: Mandate platform-neutral fallback authentication in AltID procurement language and require verification against non-Apple/Google trust anchors.
Pilot non-platform-dependent demonstrators using FIDO2/WebAuthn and OpenID Connect to validate cross-device viability.
Establish a cross-agency procurement playbook with escalation paths and diversification targets to reduce vendor concentration.
review: Platform-neutral fallback integration vs. procurement complexity. Weakness: The options fail to account for transition costs.

name: Standards-led open wallet interoperability push
consequences: Prioritizing standards-based interoperability increases alignment with European frameworks and reduces platform-specific dependencies. Downstream, this shift can accelerate portability and reduce vendor lock-in, but may slow decision cycles due to certification regimes and disagreements on profiles.
options: Advocate adoption of Danish AltID profiles aligned with FIDO2/WebAuthn and OpenID Connect to enable cross-platform compatibility.
Fund a EU-aligned interoperability project to harmonize wallet architecture and NSIS fallback mechanisms across member states.
Establish a standing standards advisory board with civil-society input to oversee interoperability certification and risk assessment.
review: Standards-based interoperability vs. certification burden. Weakness: The options fail to address certification timelines.

name: Resilience-focused supplier-diversification and continuity governance
consequences: Implement diversification requirements to limit sole dependency on a single platform vendor and to mandate alternative trust paths. Downstream, it increases bargaining power and resilience against platform policy changes, but it could impose higher initial costs and create governance complexity.
options: Institute a diversification requirement in AltID-related procurement that favors multiple independent trust anchors and avoids single-vendor dependency.
Develop formal continuity drills to simulate platform outages and policy shifts, with documented response playbooks.
Launch a cross-agency risk-sharing mechanism to fund and maintain certified fallback services.
review: Supplier-diversification vs. continuity governance. Weakness: The options fail to align with budget constraints.

name: Independent certification and evidence-demonstration program
consequences: Create credible, external validation of platform-neutral approaches and fallback paths through independent certification and public reporting. Downstream, it builds trust and reduces perceived risk, yet it may slow progress due to certification cycles and budget constraints.
options: Fund independent security and interoperability certification for fallback components with public disclosure of results.
Commission an external feasibility study and publish results to inform procurement decision-makers.
Establish an open, vendor-agnostic testing lab to reproduce MitID-like signals using non-platform hardware tokens.
review: Independent validation vs. production pace. Weakness: The options may slow progress due to review cycles.

name: Regulatory engagement and EU wallet framework alignment
consequences: Engage with regulators and EU bodies to embed platform-neutrality criteria within policy and wallet frameworks. Downstream, it aligns national efforts with European requirements and reduces regulatory risk, but it faces political resistance and competing national interests.
options: Lobby Digitaliseringsstyrelsen and EU institutions to accept platform-neutral fallback criteria within AltID designs.
Align national procurement standards with European wallet requirements and portability safeguards.
Publish a policy roadmap detailing milestones for platform neutrality within AltID and procurement.
review: Regulatory alignment vs. national autonomy. Weakness: The options overlook political resistance.

name: Public-privacy risk framing and civil society coalition strategy
consequences: Frame resilience and sovereignty in public discourse to counter platform-lock-in narratives and mobilize broad support. Downstream, it builds legitimacy and political will, but may attract adversarial messaging or misinterpretation from opponents.
options: Lead a narrative emphasizing resilience, continuity of service, and digital sovereignty in early stakeholder discussions.
Coordinate a cross-sector coalition with privacy advocates to monitor platform policy changes and respond rapidly.
Develop a transparent stakeholder map and public reporting regime to build trust and counter vendor-block arguments.
review: Public framing vs. stakeholder fatigue. Weakness: The options may over-rely on advocacy without policy commitment.
